sentences.text: Henry Fuchs.
H-E-N-R-Y and name Fuchs, F-U-C-H-S.
Hesiek.
H-E-S-I-E-K.
Tauber.
T-A-U-B-E-R.
My-- after my mother's name.
She was married to my father, Fuchsbrunner, by a rabbi, [INAUDIBLE], without registering to the-- to the-- to the-- with the registry.
So actually, this was a-- in great part, in our town, this was the custom.
That's why, officially, my name is Tauber.
But everybody knows me as Fuchsbrunner.
Fuchsbrunner.
Here, I write it F-U-C-H-S-B-R-U-N-N-E-R.
But in some places, it was written without N-N-E-R but with a M-E-R.
Fuchsbrumer.
And some other, from the same family, write with an N, Fuchsbruner.
So it's-- that's was the-- that's the story.
But I go here as 1919.
In Chrzanów, Galicia, Poland.
C-H-R-Z-A-N-O-W, O with a-- with a stripe, which means Chrzanow.
My father was born [SIGHS] in Oklusz.
Shall I spell it?
O-L-K-U-S-Z.
It was very near.
But it was-- this was-- I was Galicia, and he was Congress Poland.
Well, he moved to-- he didn't move, but he-- he came to Chrzanow at a time, by 1905, there was the Russian-Japanese war.
And at that time, [SIGHS] people who didn't want to serve in the army, they looked for a way to-- for some place where to come.
So he changed his address, and he came to Chrzanow.
And in Chrzanow, he had-- he had family in Chrzanow.
His family, his name was-- Tausowitz.
[SIGHS] Tausowitz was-- Johann Tausowitz.
He was-- he was a veterinarian.
And his wife, her name was [?
Koshitsky ?] My father's mother's name is [?
Koshitsky ?] So actually, it was family.
And this is the reason they came to Chrzanow.
And he met my mother there.
And they got married there.
I should give you a good answer. i don't have it.
But-- [LAUGHS] and I-- this is the way it was.
He came alone.
He worked in-- in leather manufacturing.
The work?
He opened up shop, and he made parts of shoes, the-- the soft parts of shoes, which at the time, whoever assembled shoes, he bought the-- the soft part already made.
And whatever they had, they made the soles, they put in the soles.
And they finished the shoe.
And this was his-- this was his profession.
He-- he sold it to-- to-- to-- not to shop, to customers, which, they made the shoes.
Pardon?
It was in Chrzanow, [SIGHS] most part in Chrzanow till the-- in the '30s.
So he opened up shop in Cracow, which was wholesale leather, just leather.
In Chrzanow later, he gave up also the parts.
But he just sold leather to the leather trade.
Which other words-- the other ones who made the parts, and the other ones who made the shoes.
They bought leather, just leather.
And this was for his profession.
And then, when my brother-- which is now in Belgium, the older brother of me, with-- with him, he put my brother-- in-- in Cracow, he opened up a wholesale place in Cracow.
Avraham Shmiel Fuchsbrunner.
Mindl Tauber.
Mindl Tauber.
My mother had-- had a profession.
She-- she had a shop.
She made shirts.
She made-- she made ladies' wears.
I don't remember that.
[LAUGHS] I was the youngest in the family.
When I [SIGHS] was a kid, she was well-established.
And she didn't work no more.
But before, when she was single, maybe-- I don't know.
Maybe she had one kid, two kids.
She still worked.
But then she gave it up.
She didn't need it.
My older sister, Golda.
Golda Lya.
Then came my brother, Bendit.
Then came my sister, Rifka Rosa, which is Regina.
Then came my brother, Reuven.
Then I came.
In public school-- public school.
It was on the-- it was two schools.
One-- one school was on the-- [SIGHS] near-- near the-- near the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the marketplace.
And one was on the [NON-ENGLISH].
I lived near the [NON-ENGLISH].
So I went to this school.
Most-- most Jewish in Chrzanow went to the other school.
Because it was near the-- more-- more-- more near the center of the town.
And I-- I was more mixed.
Yeah, Polish-- Polish more than-- than Jewish.
Well, [SIGHS] they called names.
They picked the payots.
But that's the way it was.
I took it.
No.
Back-- it was a time by the end of the year, where relatives came and waited up out of the school, and there was a fight.
They-- they-- they-- they-- they did their work, you know, because of provocation.
And then we-- we-- we had to [SIGHS] face the same-- same game.
Yeah.
Pulled by the payots, and called names, and that.
[NON-ENGLISH]
Not many.
We-- we kept together.
Jews kept together.
In the school?
The family was orthodox.
Shtebl.
[NON-ENGLISH].
There-- there was no rabbi, but there was a [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH].
That's what they called them.
He was [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] didn't-- didn't [INAUDIBLE] shtebl.
He was-- he was a [INAUDIBLE] shtebl, whatever.
In my town.
His name was Halberstam.
The person who was a rabbi in Chrzanów was Halberstam.
And the [INAUDIBLE], which was in the-- in the Radomsk shtebl was Eisenberg.
Hasidim, yeah.
Radomsk, better saying my-- I was in Radomsk.
But there was-- my father was [INAUDIBLE].
He was a-- a rabbi-- it was same Radomsk.
But he was a uncle from Radomsk rabbi.
In other words, he was a brother from Radomsk rabbi's father.
So he went to-- to this shtebl.
[INAUDIBLE] was also in Chrzanów But I was-- I was-- Chrzanów was not [INAUDIBLE].
It was a Radomsk yeshiva.
So I studied there.
And I belonged there.
When-- after-- did you study after school?
At the time of school, I went to [NON-ENGLISH].
This wasn't the time for yeshiva.
When I was school age, we learned from 8:00 till 1:00, and from-- in school, public school, every day.
But in the morning, from 6:30 till 7:30, we went to cheder.
Then we davened.
And we rushed to school 8 o'clock.
And we stood there till 1 o'clock.
And from 1:00 to 3:00 was lunch, or-- or-- or supper-- better saying supper.
There was no lunch.
It was-- was dinner.
And then, from 3 o'clock till 8 o'clock-- winter-- we went to cheder.
What-- first of all-- there was-- they called it snack.
So in order to make sure that I eat it, they [LAUGHS] actually gave me an account in the bakery.
I-- I-- went there, she gave me some [INAUDIBLE].
I buy on credit.
And because some kids like better [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know what it means-- than the [INAUDIBLE], so she made sure that I had it there.
This-- this was one way.
And the other way, gave me a sandwich.
It was a salami sandwich.
Sometimes, in the winter time, it was [NON-ENGLISH], if you know what [NON-ENGLISH] is.
So this was the way it was.
And a fruit.
Everything.
Shabbos table, Friday night-- Friday night, we came home.
Everybody has a seat.
And my father was [NON-ENGLISH].
And he sang before [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
And then he says the [NON-ENGLISH] and whatever there was, a page of saying [NON-ENGLISH].
And he gave everybody a drink.
And then it started [? seder. ?] [?
Seder, ?] Friday night, [INAUDIBLE] was a-- a main-- a main course was fish.
It was soup.
It was meat.
Usually sometimes my father got [NON-ENGLISH] and that.
And we had-- [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what it means?
Goose, goose meat, and then a compote.
And this was everything.
Did you have guests for Erev Shabbos?
Oh, yeah.
Not every week, but we had over.
From shtebl.
They-- they-- they used to come to shtebl.
And they waited till the end.
And then, they dis--
Distributed people, he asked whoever wanted to take a order.
Poor people, yeah.
We went to-- we had family in Chrzanów.
So afternoon, they came to my mother.
When I was young, they came.
And she [SIGHS] had company.
We went also to-- to-- to family.
My next family, we lived in a house when I was very young.
We lived in a-- in-- in a street.
I told you-- I mentioned it once-- it was [NON-ENGLISH], which is [INAUDIBLE], whatever.
This is the name of that street.
And in this house, we lived in one side.
And mother's sister were on the other side of-- of the-- of the corridor.
So she had also a sister.
And we had cousins, [NON-ENGLISH].
They moved-- they-- they-- they luckily-- they moved to Israel before the war, because they moved from Chrzanów to Troppau, which was Sudeten Deutschland.
And when-- when-- when the Germans came in, they-- they-- they left for Israel.
So--
Everybody was a Zionist.
Everybody was Zionist.
They were religious people.
And they had to leave.
They didn't want to stay with the Germans.
Maybe-- so they left to Israel.
And there, they stood-- one sister is still there.
One cousin, which is one-- the youngest from this family, is still alive.
The rest of them is not here.
They are not here.
True-- truly my brother-in-law, sister's husband, once, he had a-- he had a tough time here in Chrzanów.
He was [INAUDIBLE].
And he couldn't really make it out.
So he thought-- it was just a thought-- to go to Israel, as a whatever.
And he had a few dollars, whatever.
But it-- it didn't realize.
My grandmother.
Chana Henda.
My mother's mother.
Chana Henda.
She was-- she preferred to be with my mother than the aunt which was across, because she liked-- she liked better my father.
So you know, son-in-law, he was-- he was a good-- a good son-in-law.
And this was the story.
Well, [SIGHS] my grandmother had a-- had a-- had a hard life.
Her husband died when he was 30 years.
He left her with seven kids.
He died.
His name was Bendit, which my brother, his name-- my older brother's name is after this-- this grandfather.
So what was the question?
Yeah, she-- she-- she talked about family at home, how-- how-- how she provided.
And she-- she traveled from one-- from one-- from one son to the other son, to the other daughter.
This was always-- only good things.
Very much.
As a matter of fact, [LAUGHS] sometimes she didn't like it.
Because on a Saturday afternoon, [LAUGHS] I-- I-- I-- when she moved out with us, she was in a-- in a-- in a apartment from herself, I-- Shabbos, I bothered her when she was sleeping.
I-- I went for-- for whatever this is.
It used to be that-- that you go for something, like if something-- some goodies.
So this was the way it was.
But I was a kid.
That's what it was.
With everybody, I fought.
With Reuven, he was a year.
He was-- I was born July, and he was born in December.
So it was a year and a half.
So we-- we were together.
But we fought, too.
Well, I wanted to join his friends.
And he didn't want it.
So I started running.
It was all-- this-- that's how it was.
He had his friends, and I wanted to join his friends.
Kid stuff, I don't know-- played games, played-- played ball, whatever.
In school?
I played, yeah.
I did play.
I enjoyed it.
And then I played it also in cheder.
We had-- we had big-- big enjoyment.
In the winter, we had sled rides.
And after-- after-- 8 o'clock, every-- everybody was running for his enjoyment.
And we run between-- there was no-- sleds handmade.
But it worked.
And we had our enjoyment.
We came home.
We had to make the homework and go back to school.
And this was-- this was the after day.
That's-- that's how it was.
Not really.
The only-- the only way, when I went away from my town, was-- the customary was everybody was-- had to register for-- for-- for-- for the military.
This was by the year of-- by the age of 17, register.
And then they called you in by-- by 21, by 20, 21, in this year, in this age.
You had to stay for commission-- for commission.
And it was customary the Jewish people didn't want to go to-- to-- to the army.
And they looked for a way not to go.
So when my turn came, I had to leave town.
And I left home to Cracow.
And I stood there, hidden.
I didn't-- I didn't go to-- to-- to-- I didn't present myself.
So I was illegal in Cracow.
And my father sent me money, whatever, you know?
Where did you hide?
I didn't hide.
I didn't hide.
I was in Cracow, with a-- with a-- with another friend, who also run from-- from Chrzanów.
This was customary.
I-- I-- got-- my father gave me money to rent-- to rent something.
I slept in a-- in a-- in a house, where there were other people.
So they gave me a bed.
And then I slept there.
And then I lived there.
And this was going on until January, '39.
Why?
For two reasons.
One reason was a Jew was very much [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] fragile about the kids, you know?
They-- they didn't want to give them the rough-- rough time.
And secondly, we weren't so much-- what do you call it-- citizens, good to Poland, that we should serve the army.
They weren't so good to us that we should serve their army.
It was my homeland.
I was born there.
I was educated.
I went to school there and-- and-- and we had-- we had a business there.
But [SIGHS] friendship-- there was no friendship.
There was no friendship.
My hometown was-- it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it-- a nice town.
We had, I would say, 15,000, 20,000 Jews living there.
We were 2/3, 2/3.
And we had-- we had-- I have a book here.
You can see that-- that there was very much a social life, very much education-- education life.
And everything was there, really everything.
We had a library.
If you ask me a library in school, we had our-- our books.
And-- and-- and in shtebl, there was a library that you wouldn't fit into this house.
Of what?
The name of it, [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], if you know what it is.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And it was a-- it was a lot going on.
[NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] There was still the-- it was a-- it was-- it was a library.
You could study there 24 hours a day and-- and never-- never get tired of it.
There was sometimes came-- came a-- a troop to Chrzanów.
[NON-ENGLISH].
It came.
They came.
As a child, not-- not-- not in a theater.
I was--I was in a group-- we didn't go to theaters.
But we went-- sometimes there was a Purim thing, or Hanukkah.
And it was-- it was-- a group came to-- just to make-- make a party.
So I went there.
Yeah.
This was customary.
Yeah, customary-- we did [NON-ENGLISH].
We did all the things, which was-- we did even annoy people in [INAUDIBLE] to throw them those things which annoys them.
I feel sorry for them, but that-- this was the custom.
You know, this-- this-- those little [INAUDIBLE] in the beards?
This was fun.
But this-- this-- this was a part of-- of-- of the-- of the fun.
But we did it.
Yeah.
You-- elementary school before.
Oh, it was-- it was 13. 11, 12, 13.
I studied.
The most thing, I studied a few years in-- in the shtebl.
I belonged there.
I belonged there.
It was-- I learned [NON-ENGLISH] for a few years.
Then I went to Sosnowice and [NON-ENGLISH], which I studied there for a year and a half.
Oh yeah, I sleep in the town.
And-- and some people-- ate [INAUDIBLE].
You know, [INAUDIBLE] One day here, one day here.
My father-- I was in-- in a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]?
A-- a-- a place with a cook, where people went there every there.
And the rabbi take-- took care of it.
Because the reason was, my father was known to-- to rabbi's son-in-law.
The son-in-law was-- was a-- was a son of-- of [INAUDIBLE].
And my father was-- was a-- was a [NON-ENGLISH].
If you know what it means.
So he took me to the [NON-ENGLISH].
So I didn't [INAUDIBLE].
But we had boys in Chrzanów from the yeshiva, which they ate by us.
One day, one, one-- another day, another one.
This is the most customary.
Oh, yes.
[SIGHS] I knew a lot of people.
But I should tell you, if I take now the Chrzanów book, and I look up the people there, I didn't-- I didn't-- I didn't-- I didn't know many of them.
I knew more-- more in my-- in my capacity where they were.
There were, for example, organizations, [NON-ENGLISH].
They were-- there was a-- one was a Poliziani.
There was-- one was a social-- a Bund, and this.
I didn't belong there.
So I belonged more to the-- to the Haggadah, to the more religious ones.
And that's what it was.
So now, from this point, you went to Cracow.
Your father is helping you in Cracow.
[SIGHS]
Yeah.
Yeah, he helped me out, and I should-- I should be able to stay there.
OK.
